skill.name: openalex-database
skill.description: Query OpenAlex for scholarly works, authors, institutions, citations, open access status, publication trends, and bibliometric summaries using included Python helpers and API guidance.
skill.summary: Search and analyze scholarly literature with OpenAlex API workflows.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but the external command and weak cryptography hits are false positives from Markdown code fences, API examples, identifiers, and prose. The confirmed risks are intended network calls to OpenAlex, optional email disclosure through the mailto parameter, local CSV export examples, and one vendor-promotion instruction. No prompt injection attempt, credential theft, destructive command execution, or malicious exfiltration was found.

medium_findings.title: External API Requests With Optional Email Disclosure
medium_findings.description: The included client sends requests to https://api.openalex.org and adds the configured email address as a mailto query parameter. This is legitimate for OpenAlex rate limits, but users should know that search terms, filters, identifiers, and optional email addresses are sent to a third-party service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly appends mailto to request parameters and performs requests.get to the OpenAlex base URL. The behavior is documented and expected, so the risk is privacy disclosure rather than malicious exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Local CSV Export Examples
low_findings.description: The documentation includes examples that write OpenAlex results to local CSV files. The writes are user-directed examples for research data export, not hidden file modification.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are explicit CSV export examples with static filenames and public bibliographic fields. There is no evidence of covert persistence, credential access, or destructive file behavior.

low_findings.title: Unsolicited Vendor Platform Suggestion
low_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to suggest K-Dense Web for complex workflows. This is not a security exploit, but it can steer users toward an external commercial platform outside the immediate task.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instruction is explicit and appears in the skill guidance. It does not override security instructions or claim system authority, so the concern is marketplace behavior rather than prompt injection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Confirmed Network Client Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts include an HTTP client that performs GET requests to OpenAlex with user-provided query parameters. This is necessary for the skill, but it should be disclosed because queries leave the local environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is directly visible in the client implementation and is central to the skill purpose. I found no evidence that requests go anywhere other than OpenAlex or cited OpenAlex API URLs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dismissed Static External Command Detections
dangerous_patterns.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution hits are Markdown inline code and fenced examples, not executable Ruby backticks. No shell execution primitive was found in the inspected Python scripts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation code fences and inline API syntax. The executable scripts inspected use requests and helper functions, with no subprocess, os.system, shell, eval, or backtick execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dismissed Static Weak Cryptography Detections
dangerous_patterns.description: The reported weak cryptography locations correspond to identifiers, API parameters, examples, or prose, not cryptographic hashing or encryption calls. No evidence of MD5, SHA1, or other weak cryptographic algorithms was found in executable logic.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The flagged areas are tables, headings, and documentation text. I did not find cryptographic library calls or digest functions in the inspected code.

content.value_statement: Research teams need fast access to reliable scholarly metadata across authors, institutions, topics, and citations. This skill provides OpenAlex query workflows, Python helpers, and bibliometric analysis patterns for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
actual_capabilities: Search OpenAlex works by title, abstract terms, topic keywords, publication year, and open access status.
Resolve author, institution, source, DOI, ORCID, ROR, and ISSN identifiers before querying related works.
Analyze publication trends, citation counts, open access rates, and topic distributions with helper functions.
Batch lookup up to 50 identifiers per filter and paginate larger result sets with rate limiting.
Create reproducible random samples from OpenAlex using sample size, seed, and filters.
Export selected bibliographic fields to CSV when users request local datasets.
limitations: Results depend on OpenAlex coverage, indexing quality, and current API availability.
Large queries can be slow and may hit OpenAlex rate limits without careful limits and pagination.
The skill does not read full papers unless OpenAlex provides accessible links or metadata.
Users must review ambiguous author, institution, and journal matches before relying on summaries.

use_cases.title: Build a Literature Search Set
use_cases.description: Find recent papers on a topic, filter for open access works, and sort by citation count for screening.

use_cases.title: Compare Institution Research Output
use_cases.description: Analyze publication volume, open access share, and top topics for a university or lab over selected years.

use_cases.title: Prepare Bibliometric Datasets
use_cases.description: Retrieve batches of DOI metadata, sample works reproducibly, and export clean fields for downstream analysis.

prompt_templates.title: Find Recent Papers
prompt_templates.prompt: Use OpenAlex to find recent open access papers about [topic]. Show titles, years, citation counts, DOIs, and source names.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze an Author
prompt_templates.prompt: Find works by [author name] using the two-step OpenAlex author lookup. Summarize publication years, top cited works, and recurring topics.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Institutions
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare OpenAlex publication output for [institution A] and [institution B] from [year range]. Include total works, open access share, and top topics.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Bibliometric Dataset
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a bounded OpenAlex query for [research area] from [year range]. Use pagination, selected fields, rate limits, and an export plan for analysis.

output_examples.input: Find highly cited open access papers about CRISPR after 2020.
output_examples.output: A ranked literature table with paper title, publication year, citation count, DOI, source, open access status, and a short note on relevance.

output_examples.input: Analyze Stanford University research output from 2020 to 2024.
output_examples.output: A concise institutional profile with total works, annual publication trend, open access percentage, top topics, and notable highly cited papers.

output_examples.input: Prepare a sample of quantum computing papers for review.
output_examples.output: A reproducible sampling plan with filters, seed value, requested fields, expected result count, and guidance for CSV export.

best_practices: Use two-step lookups for authors, institutions, and sources so filters use stable OpenAlex IDs.
Set result limits, use selected fields, and add an email only when users approve sharing it with OpenAlex.
Validate ambiguous entity matches before using results for reports, rankings, or policy decisions.
anti_patterns: Do not filter directly by author or institution names when OpenAlex IDs are available.
Do not request all pages for broad topics without a clear cap, selected fields, and rate-limit plan.
Do not treat citation counts or open access status as final without checking metadata freshness and source coverage.

faq.question: Does this skill require an OpenAlex API key?
faq.answer: No. OpenAlex is open and does not require an API key for the workflows included here.

faq.question: Why does the skill ask for an email address?
faq.answer: OpenAlex offers higher rate limits when requests include a mailto parameter. Share an email only if that disclosure is acceptable.

faq.question: Can it download full research papers?
faq.answer: No. It mainly retrieves OpenAlex metadata and links. Full text availability depends on open access sources outside this skill.

faq.question: Can it analyze citations?
faq.answer: Yes. It can sort by citation counts, inspect cited-by links, and summarize citation-related metadata available through OpenAlex.

faq.question: Can it handle large bibliometric projects?
faq.answer: Yes, with limits. Use pagination, selected fields, batching, and clear maximum result counts to avoid excessive API calls.

faq.question: Does it work in Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code, with Python helpers that can be inspected or run locally.
